skill.name: error-diagnostics-error-trace
skill.description: Guides implementation of error tracking, structured logging, alerting, error grouping, recovery patterns, and monitoring dashboards.
skill.summary: Build and improve production error tracking and observability workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution, weak-crypto, env-access, and critical heuristic findings are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, JavaScript template literals, and documentation text. The skill is legitimate observability guidance, but it includes examples for outbound error telemetry, alert webhooks, broad user context capture, and local log files. Publish with a privacy and network warning, not as a blocked or malicious skill.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Error Telemetry Examples
medium_findings.description: The custom tracker example posts serialized error events to a configurable endpoint with a bearer API key. This is expected for observability, but copied code could transmit stack traces, request context, or user data outside the project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call and Authorization header are explicit. The endpoint is configurable and legitimate for monitoring, so this is a privacy and network risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Broad User and Runtime Context Capture
medium_findings.description: The examples add user identifiers, user segment data, promise details, runtime metadata, request URLs, IP addresses, user agents, and stack traces to logs or error events. This may expose sensitive data if teams do not minimize and sanitize telemetry.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented fields are visible in the examples and commonly contain personal or sensitive operational data. The examples include some redaction, but not for all captured context.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Alert Webhook Posting
medium_findings.description: The Slack alert channel posts alert payloads to a configured webhook URL. This is normal alerting behavior, but it sends service, environment, severity, and metric details to an external destination.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The aiohttp POST to webhook_url is explicit and semantically part of alert delivery. The destination is user configured, so it is not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings occur at Markdown code fence boundaries, JavaScript template literals, or resource references. No executable skill script or command invocation was found at the listed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed these locations are documentation examples or Markdown references. They do not execute commands when the skill is loaded.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The listed weak-cryptography locations do not show a weak hash or cipher. The playbook uses SHA-256 only for non-security error grouping, and the flagged lines are documentation text or key-name matching.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited static line is a sensitive-key redaction check, not cryptography. The only visible hash use is SHA-256 for grouping, not password storage or security decisions.

low_findings.title: Static Environment Access Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported environment-access lines reference user context callbacks and a sensitive-key denylist, not environment variable reads. They still relate to telemetry privacy, which is covered by the medium findings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines do not use process.env or similar APIs. The nearby code handles user context and redaction rather than credential retrieval.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: No prompt injection phrases, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, or review-bypass instructions were found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review and keyword search did not identify prompt injection language. This is a negative finding based on the available files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Telemetry Sink
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code sends captured error events to a configurable HTTP endpoint using fetch.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound POST is explicit and carries serialized events. The risk depends on endpoint governance and sanitization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Webhook Alert Delivery
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code posts alert payloads to a configured Slack webhook URL.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network call is direct and intentional. It is legitimate alerting behavior but still transmits operational metadata externally.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Codebase and Log File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read source files for analysis and write application logs to local log files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read and log file transport examples are explicit. They are common observability practices but require scope control.

content.value_statement: Production errors are hard to triage when logs, alerts, and context are fragmented. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical observability patterns for faster diagnosis.
actual_capabilities: Assess current error handling, logging coverage, and monitoring gaps.
Guide setup for Sentry-style tracking and custom error event pipelines.
Design structured logging with request, audit, error, and performance context.
Define alert rules for error rate, latency, memory, and disk conditions.
Recommend error grouping, retry, circuit breaker, and recovery patterns.
Outline dashboard views for trends, top errors, users affected, and live streams.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples; it does not deploy monitoring services by itself.
Telemetry examples need privacy review before use with production user data.
Generated thresholds require tuning against real service behavior and traffic volume.
Some examples are illustrative and may need updates for current library versions.

use_cases.title: Add Error Tracking to a Web Service
use_cases.description: Plan an error tracking rollout with capture points, metadata, filtering, and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Improve Production Alert Quality
use_cases.description: Convert noisy error signals into actionable alert rules with cooldowns and clear severity levels.

use_cases.title: Create an Error Operations Dashboard
use_cases.description: Design dashboard sections for trends, top errors, affected users, active alerts, and live streams.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of an existing service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my service for error tracking gaps. Focus on missing capture points, weak logging, alert coverage, and safe next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Sentry Integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate setup for a Node or Python service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an implementation plan for adding Sentry-style error tracking to this service, including filtering, user context, sampling, and validation.

prompt_templates.title: Design Alert Rules
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced alert tuning for production systems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design alert rules for error rate, latency, memory pressure, and disk space. Include thresholds, windows, cooldowns, channels, and runbook notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Observability Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Propose an error observability architecture with structured logging, event transport, grouping, dashboards, privacy controls, and incident response workflows.

output_examples.input: A team needs better visibility into unhandled exceptions in a production API.
output: A capture plan for global exceptions, rejected promises, request middleware, and worker failures.
Recommended metadata fields with sensitive data removed before transport.
Verification steps for test errors, alert delivery, and dashboard visibility.

output_examples.input: An on-call team receives too many low-value error alerts.
output: Alert rules grouped by severity, time window, and escalation channel.
Cooldown and deduplication recommendations to reduce repeated pages.
A review checklist for threshold tuning after the first week of data.

output_examples.input: A service needs a dashboard for error trends and affected users.
output: A dashboard layout with error rate, top errors, affected users, MTTR, and active alerts.
Suggested filters for environment, release, service, and time range.
Privacy notes for limiting user identifiers and request details.

best_practices: Define telemetry privacy rules before sending stack traces or request data to external tools.
Start with conservative alert thresholds, then tune them using real production baselines.
Validate every integration with test errors, failed transports, and dashboard checks.
anti_patterns: Do not send raw request bodies, cookies, passwords, tokens, or secrets in error events.
Do not page on every exception without grouping, sampling, severity, and cooldown rules.
Do not copy example integrations into production without reviewing endpoints and data retention.

faq.question: Does this skill install an error tracking service?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance, examples, checklists, and review criteria for your chosen tools.

faq.question: Which monitoring tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Sentry-style tracking, structured logging, Slack alerts, dashboards, and custom event pipelines.

faq.question: Can it help reduce noisy alerts?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests thresholds, windows, cooldowns, grouping, severity levels, and escalation channels.

faq.question: Is it safe for production telemetry?
faq.answer: Use it with review. The examples need endpoint controls, redaction, sampling, and privacy checks before production use.

faq.question: Can it analyze a codebase?
faq.answer: It includes example approaches for scanning error handling and logging patterns across project files.

faq.question: What should I provide in my prompt?
faq.answer: Provide the service language, framework, current logging setup, monitoring tools, alert channels, and privacy constraints.
